_definitions.id: Unique identifier for each component in the entire schematic.
_definitions.refdes: Reference Designator (e.g., U15, P5, R1). Identifies the component type and sequence number.
_definitions.value: The practical value or part number of the component (e.g., '100K, 1%', 'SN74LVC...').
_definitions.footprint: The physical footprint/package type used on the PCB (e.g., '402', 'QFN32').
_definitions.pins: List of all terminal pins available on the component.
_definitions.pin: The specific numbering or identifier of a physical pin on the component.
_definitions.net: The signal net name this pin is connected to. Pins with the same net are electrically tied together.
_definitions.name: The specific functional name or role of the pin (e.g., 'GND', 'VCC', 'TX', 'RX').
_definitions.bom_value: The component value as listed in the Bill of Materials.
_definitions.bom_description: A detailed description of the component from the Bill of Materials.
_definitions.part_number: The specific manufacturer or distributor part number.
_definitions.package: The physical packaging or housing of the component.
_definitions.pinmux: Pin multiplexing configurations for processor pins, detailing the signal mapped in active modes.
_definitions.design_rationale: Engineering design notes and reasoning explaining the architectural choices for the specific IC.
_definitions.nets_info: Detailed information regarding power and signal nets, storing connected pins and metadata.
_definitions.power_specs: Electrical specifications for power nets, including Default Voltage and Maximum Current outputs.
_definitions.connected_pins: A list of pins (in RefDes.Pin format) physically attached to the specified net.

components.design_rationale: Voltage supervisor / reset generator: monitors VDD_3V3B power rail and generates active-low SYS_RESETn (system reset for AM3358) and ETH_RESETn (Ethernet PHY U14 reset). Ensures proper power-on reset sequencing.

design_rationale.3.4.3 Apply Power: Connect microHDMI Cable to the Board** The connector is fairly robust, but we suggest that you not use the cable as a leash for your Beagle.
design_rationale.5.4 Power Management: The **TPS65217C** power management device is used along with a separate LDO to provide power to the system. The **TPS65217C** version provides for the proper voltages required for the DDR3L.
design_rationale.5.8 Power Sources: And, at that point the PC most likely will complain, but you can also use a dual connector USB cable to the PC to get to 1A. The power supply is not provided with the board but can be easily obtained from numerous sources.
design_rationale.5.10 Power Button: A power button is provided near the reset button close to the Ethernet connector. This button takes advantage of the input to the PMIC for power down features.
design_rationale.6.1 Power Section: High Level Power Block Diagram** This section describes the power section of the design and all the functions performed by the **TPS65217C**.
design_rationale.6.1.3 USB Power: When powering from the USB port, the VDD\_5V rail is not provided to the expansion headers, so capes that require the 5V rail to supply the cape direct, bypassing the **TPS65217C**, will not have that rail available for use. The 5VDC supply from the USB port is provided on the SYS\_5V, the one that comes from the **TPS65217C**, rail of the expansion header for use by a cape.
design_rationale.6.1.4 Power Selection: The selection of either the 5VDC or the USB as the power source is handled internally to the **TPS65217C** and automatically switches to 5VDC power if both are connected. SW can change the power configuration via the I2C interface from the processor.
design_rationale.6.1.5 Power Button: This is a momentary switch, the same type of switch used for reset and boot selection on the board. Pressing the power button will cause the board to power up again if the processor puts the board in the power off mode.
design_rationale.6.1.7 Power Consumption: The power consumption of the board varies based on power scenarios and the board boot processes. Measurements were taken with the board in the following configuration: - DC powered and USB powered - HDMI monitor connected - USB HUB - 4GB Thumbdrive - Ethernet connected @ 100M - Serial debug cable connected **Table 4** is an analysis of the power consumption of the board in these various scenarios.
design_rationale.6.1.9 Power Rails: **Figure 25** shows the connections of each of the rails from the **TPS65217C**.
design_rationale.*6.1.9.8 Power Sequencing*: This diagram is used elsewhere to convey additional information.
design_rationale.6.1.10 Power LED: If you ever see the LED flash once, that means that the **TPS65217C** started the process and encountered an issue that caused it to shut down.
design_rationale.6.1.11 TPS65217C Power Up Process: At power up all switchers and LDOs are off except for the **VRTC LDO** (1.8V), which provides power to the VRTC rail and controls the **RTC\_PORZn** input pin to the processor, which starts the power up process of the processor. The **LDO\_PGOOD** signal is provided by the **TPS65217C** to the processor.
design_rationale.6.1.13 Low Power Mode Support: This section covers three general power down modes that are available. These modes are only described from a Hardware perspective as it relates to the HW design.
design_rationale.6.2.8 Power Rails: The **DDR3L** memory device and the DDR3 rails on the processor are supplied by the **TPS65217C**. Default voltage is 1.5V but can be scaled down to 1.35V if desired.
design_rationale.6.9.3 Ethernet PHY Power, Reset, and Clo: Figure 42 shows the power, reset, and lock connections to the LAN8710A PHY. Each of these areas is discussed in more detail in the following sections.
design_rationale.6.10.7 Power Connections: A filter is provided to minimize any noise from the 1.8V rail getting back into the device. HDMI Power Connections All of the interfaces between the processor and the **TDA19988** are 3.3V tolerant allowing for direct connection.
design_rationale.6.11.1 Power Switch: **U8** is a switch that allows the power to the connector to be turned on or off by the processor.
design_rationale.7.2 Power Jack: This uses the same power connector as is used on the original BeagleBone. 5VDC Power Jack** The board requires a regulated 5VDC +/-.25V supply at 1A.
design_rationale.8.6 Cape Power: This section describes the power rails for the capes and their usage.
design_rationale.8.6.1 Main Board Power: If the power requirement for the capes exceeds the current rating, then locally generated voltage rail can be used. It is recommended that this rail be used to power any buffers or level translators that may be used.
design_rationale.8.6.2 Expansion Board External Power: It is possible to provide 5V to the main board from an expansion board. **There are several precautions that need to me taken when working with the expansion headers to prevent damage to the board.** - **1) Do not apply any voltages to any I/O pins when the board is not powered on.** - **2) Do not drive any external signals into the I/O pins until after the VDD\_3V3B rail is up.** - **3) Do not apply any voltages that are generated from external sources.** - **4) If voltages are generated from the VDD\_5V signal, those supplies must not become active until after the VDD\_3V3B rail is up.** - **5) If you are applying signals from other boards into the expansion headers, make sure you power the board up after you power up the BeagleBone Black or make the connections after power is applied on both boards.** **Powering the processor via its I/O pins can cause damage to the processor.**

design_decisions: Selected as the dedicated Power Management IC (PMIC) (TPS65217) representing the core of the BeagleBone power tree.
Integrates three high-efficiency step-down converters (for VDD_CORE, VDDS_DDR, VDCDC2) and multiple LDOs to precisely meet AM3358 power sequencing requirements.
Manges automatic power path selection and battery charging, deciding seamlessly between 5V DC jack input and USB_DC input.
failure_modes: A 12V input directly into the 5V DC Jack (P1) exceeds the PMIC's 6.5V absolute maximum rating, causing catastrophic breakdown of internal FETs.
Thermal shutdown will trigger if output rails (like VDDS_DDR) are shorted to ground, dropping PMIC_PGOOD and asserting SYS_RESETn continuously.
If I2C0 communication lines are severed/shorted, the AM3358 cannot dynamically scale VDD_CORE (SmartReflex DVS), causing potential software instability under heavy load.

components.design_rationale: Open-drain inverter: generates eMMC_RSTn (active-low reset for eMMC U13) from CPU control signal. Connected via R162 to U13 pin K5.

design_decisions: External TL5209 LDO utilized specifically to generate VDD_3V3B instead of exclusively relying on PMIC internal LDOs.
Ensures a dedicated, noise-isolated 3.3V power rail for critical analog and isolated peripheral subsystems.

components.design_rationale: AM3358 Sitara ARM Cortex-A8 processor: main SoC running at up to 1GHz. Interfaces: DDR3 (U12), eMMC (U13), Ethernet MII (U14), HDMI via TDA19988 (U11), USB0 client (P4), USB1 host (P3), I2C (PMIC U2, EEPROM U7, HDMI U11), UART0 debug (J1), expansion headers P8/P9.

software_bindings.default_pinmux: Defined in mux.c: uart0_pin_mux (RXACTIVE | PULLUP_EN), mmc0_pin_mux, rgmii1_pin_mux, i2c0_pin_mux.
software_bindings.device_tree_constraints: Defined in am335x-bone-common.dtsi: dcdc3_reg (VDD_CORE) constrained to 0.925V - 1.15V. Memory mapped at 0x80000000 (256MB).

design_decisions: Chosen as the core SoC (System on Chip) driving the BeagleBone Black (TI Sitara AM3358).
Features an ARM Cortex-A8 core, dual Programmable Real-Time Units (PRUs) for real-time deterministic I/O control, and an SGX530 3D graphics engine.
Hardware boot configuration relies heavily on the state of the LCD_DATA pins sampled during power-on-reset.
failure_modes: Overvoltage on any 3.3V GPIO pin beyond the AM3358 absolute max rating while the board is unpowered will leak current into VDD_3V3A via internal clamping diodes (back-powering), potentially damaging the IO ring.
If VDD_CORE drops below specification during execution, the CPU will freeze or trigger a kernel panic due to logic gate propagation delay failures.

components.bom_description: SINGLE  POSITIVE-EDGE-TRIGGERED  D-TYPE  FLIP-FLOP WITH  CLEAR  AND  PRESET
components.design_rationale: D flip-flop used in HDMI clock generation circuit. Q output (pin 5) connects to 12MHz clock path (R21) for TDA19988 HDMI transmitter (U11). All inputs (CLK, D, Q_BAR) tied to GND, PRE_BAR and CLR_BAR tied to VDD_3V3A.
EEPROM for Board ID and Cape identification

USB power distribution switch (TPS2051B): controls 5V VBUS power delivery to USB1 Host connector P3. Enabled by USB1_DRVVBUS signal from AM3358. Provides overcurrent protection for external USB devices.
USB1 Host ESD protection array (TPD4S012): protects USB1_DP/DM differential data lines and VBUS from electrostatic discharge at USB Host connector P3.
USB0 (Client) ESD protection and VBUS clamping.

design_decisions: Selected because the AM3358 lacks a native built-in HDMI PHY; the TDA19988 serves as an external parallel-RGB to HDMI framer.
Interfaces with the CPU via the 16-bit LCD_DATA bus and requires external I2C (I2C0) for Display Data Channel (DDC) EDID communication with monitors.
failure_modes: Plugging an actively powered generic un-isolated HDMI cable into P6 can cause severe ESD discharge. If it surpasses D6/D7 clamping ratings, the internal TMDS transceivers of U11 will arc and fail.
If the PTC thermal fuse RT1 on DVI_+5V trips to open-circuit due to an external monitor short, HDMI devices will fail to detect the BeagleBone via Hot-Plug Detect (HPD).

design_rationale.5.3 Memory: Described in the following sections are the three memory devices found on the board.
design_rationale.6.2.6 Memory Device: The design supports the standard DDR3 and DDR3L x16 devices and is built using the DDR3L. A single x16 device is used on the board and there is no support for two x8 devices.
design_rationale.6.2.7 DDR3L Memory Design: **Address Lines:** Provide the row address for ACTIVATE commands, and the column address and auto pre-charge bit (A10) for READ/WRITE commands, to select one location out of the memory array in the respective bank. The address inputs also provide the op-code during a LOAD MODE command.
design_rationale.6.3 4GB eMMC Memory: Cards use different controllers and different memories, all of which can have bad locations that the controller handles. The following sections describe the design and device that is used on the board to implement this interface.

design_decisions: A single 16-bit wide chip (MT41K256M16) is utilized to optimize PCB layout space, simplify single-chip termination networks, and reduce BOM cost compared to dual 8-bit chip layouts.

components.design_rationale: Onboard non-volatile memory for OS boot
design_decisions: Provides 4GB of on-board eMMC non-volatile storage, operating on standard MMC/eMMC protocols via the MMC1 structural interface.
Empowers the board to boot an OS natively without depending on a slower, external microSD card, enabling better boot reliability.

components.bom_description: Ethernet ICs 10/100 Ethernet XCVR w/HPAutoMDIX FlexPwr

design_rationale.4. Connect the Ethernet Cable: If you decide you want to connect to your local area network, an Ethernet cable can be used.
design_rationale.6.9 10/100 Ethernet: It uses the same PHY as is used on the original BeagleBone.
design_rationale.6.9.1 Ethernet Processor Interface: Ethernet Processor Interface This is the same interface as is used on the BeagleBone.
design_rationale.6.9.2 Ethernet Connector Interface: Ethernet Connector Interface This is the same interface as is used on the BeagleBone.
design_rationale.6.9.3 Ethernet PHY Power, Reset, and Clo: Figure 42 shows the power, reset, and lock connections to the LAN8710A PHY. Each of these areas is discussed in more detail in the following sections.
design_rationale.7.8 Ethernet: Ethernet Connector** The PHY supports AutoMDX which means either a straight or a swap cable can be used

design_decisions: Selected as the 10/100 Ethernet PHY (LAN8710A) due to its small footprint, hardware strapping capabilities, and reliable MII interface support.
Requires a dedicated 25MHz external crystal (Y3) for its internal PLL to generate stable TX/RX clocks since the CPU is not providing an external synchronous clock.
failure_modes: If the ETH_RESETn supervisor circuit (U1) fails to pull HIGH, the PHY remains in infinite reset, rendering the Ethernet interface invisible to the AM3358 MAC.
If Y3 crystal fails, the PHY loses its internal clock, resulting in immediate link drop and no MDIO communication.

components.design_rationale: Dual non-inverting buffer for UART0 debug interface: buffers/isolates UART0_TX and UART0_RX signals between AM3358 (U5) and debug serial header J1 (B_UART0_TX/RX). Always enabled (OE tied to GND/VDD).
Open-drain inverter: converts LDO_PGOOD (active-high power-good from TPS65217C) into SYS_RESETn (active-low system reset). Keeps AM3358 in reset until all power rails are stable.

Power_Management.description: Troubleshooting sequence for the main Power Tree and TPS65217C PMIC.
test_procedure: 1. Measure 5V input from DC Jack at P1 or USB_DC at P4.
2. Check SYS_5V system rail to ensure power path FETs (internal to PMIC) are active.
3. Measure PMIC_PGOOD (U2 pin 26) to verify all PMIC internal DCDC and LDO outputs are within regulation.
5. Measure SYS_RESETn to ensure the U1 voltage supervisor released the board from reset.
6. Measure VDD_3V3A, VDDS_DDR, and VDD_CORE individually if PMIC_PGOOD is LOW.

Ethernet_Interface.description: Troubleshooting sequence for LAN8710A PHY and RJ45 link issues.
test_procedure: 1. Check 3.3V power at VDD_3V3B isolating the PHY.
2. Verify ETH_RESETn is HIGH (released) at LAN8710A Pin 19.
3. Probe Y3 (25MHz) crystal with an oscilloscope to ensure the PHY PLL is oscillating.
4. Measure MII TX/RX clock lines to confirm AM3358 and LAN8710A MAC-to-PHY communication.
5. Check RJ45 LEDs and PHY bootstrap resistor voltages at power-up to confirm PHY address (0x0).

Boot_Failure.description: Troubleshooting sequence when the board powers on but LEDs do not flash (no bootloader execution).
test_procedure: 1. Verify SYS_5V and VDD_3V3A rails are stable.
3. Measure Y2 (24MHz) main crystal oscillator. If dead, the AM3358 will not execute ROM code.
4. Check the logical state of LCD_DATA[15:0] pins immediately after SYS_RESETn goes HIGH to ensure correct SYSBOOT configuration.
5. Monitor UART0_TX at header J1 (Debug Serial) for 'CCCCC' or U-Boot early printk output.
6. Verify MMC1_CLK and MMC1_CMD for eMMC activity.

_engineering_notes.ddr3_routing_rules: From sprabu5.md: DDR3 traces must be strictly length-matched. VREF must be exactly half of VDDS_DDR isolated by ground.
_engineering_notes.power_delivery_network: From sprabu5.md: Decoupling capacitors (0.1uF, 10uF) must be placed as close to AM3358 power pins as physically possible to minimize parasitic inductance.
_engineering_notes.silicon_errata: From sprz360i.md: Known AM3358 bugs include USB Boot ROM Code using default DATAPOLARITY, and DDR3 JEDEC maximum self-refresh command limit exceptions.
